title: The Most Important Gates in Quantum Computing Explained
summary: IBM Technology explains quantum computing gates, covering qubits, superposition, and entanglement. The video details how Hadamard and CNOT gates shape quantum circuits and their importance in quantum computation.
writeup: This talk walks through the three quantum gates that together define what makes quantum computing fundamentally different from classical computation: the Hadamard gate (superposition), the CNOT gate (entanglement), and the T gate (phase). The mathematical derivations are shown explicitly, from Dirac notation and matrix multiplication through to the Bell state and the Gottesman-Knill theorem. The through-line is a precise answer to why Clifford circuits alone are classically simulable, and what the T gate adds that crosses into true quantum advantage.

key_points: The Hadamard gate, a 2x2 matrix (1/sqrt(2)) [[1,1],[1,-1]], transforms the ground state |0> into an equal superposition of |0> and |1>, each with probability 1/2, which is verified by squaring the coefficient 1/sqrt(2) to get 1/2.

Applying a 4x4 expanded Hadamard followed by the 4x4 CNOT gate to the two-qubit |00> state produces the Bell state (1/sqrt(2))(|00> + |11>), one of the most studied equations in physics and the mathematical signature of entanglement.

Bell's theorem shows that the Bell state forces the universe to abandon local realism: quantum states do not have definite properties prior to measurement, a consequence encoded directly in the structure of the Bell state derivation.

The Clifford group, which includes both the Hadamard and CNOT gates, is insufficient for universal quantum computation. The Gottesman-Knill theorem proves that Clifford-only circuits can be efficiently simulated on a classical computer despite producing superposition and entanglement.

The T gate, which rotates the phase of |1> by e^(i*pi/4) without changing measurement probabilities, is what breaks classical tractability: it adds a dimension to the state space, causes simulation costs to grow exponentially, and together with the Clifford group achieves universality.
Clifford plus T gates form a universal gate set, meaning any quantum evolution that nature allows can be approximated to arbitrary precision with these three gates alone.

chapters.title: Linear Algebra of Qubits and Gates
